OPTIMIZATION Perf Tiers GPU vs CPU Optim Rules Scalability
ENGINE INTERNALS Data Interfaces Engine Gotchas
GitHub
● Niagara · Custom HLSL · GPU Particle Simulation

Niagara HLSL Reference

Engine: UE 5.x
Context: Scratch Pad · Stateless Modules
Stage: Spawn · Update · Render
⚗ HLSL Reference
Mental Model — Where · When · What Costs
Orient in 30 seconds

Niagara runs per-particle scripts on either the CPU or the GPU. Each emitter has a Spawn script (runs once per new particle) and an Update script (runs every frame, per particle). Data Interfaces are the only bridge from your script to engine data — textures, skeletal meshes, distance fields, other systems. Everything else is compute-isolated: no scene queries, no callbacks, no game-thread access from GPU sims.

Where code runs
CPU simFull module set, gameplay callbacks, light emission. Scales with particle count on the game thread — keep under 100 particles per emitter.
GPU simCompute dispatch, near-zero CPU cost. No callbacks, no per-particle lights, no sync physics. Right for 100+ particles.
Module scriptsStateless HLSL in Scratch Pad — inputs via pins, outputs via pin writes. No return, no globals.
Hybrid via DIData Interface calls (Texture Sample, Skeletal Mesh, Distance Field, Niagara DI) are the only way a script reads engine state.
When it runs
PrePhysicsDefault tick group. Spawn + Update run before scene physics — safe for most work.
DuringPhysicsRuns parallel with physics. Avoid unless you specifically need async overlap.
PostPhysicsRuns after collisions resolve. Needed when reading post-physics transforms (e.g. ragdolls).
PostOpaqueRenderGPU-only stage. Lets sim sample the scene depth buffer for collision or soft-depth. 1-frame lag vs CPU.
4 things that control cost
→?Particle count — the multiplier on everything else. Tier CPU at <100, GPU at 100-1M.
→?Script complexity — ops per particle per frame. Expensive intrinsics (pow, texture reads) hurt more on GPU when fanned across millions of threads.
→EData Interface fan-out — Skeletal Mesh DI binds 5+ SRVs per emitter; Texture Sample DI is cheap; Render Target DI burns VRAM.
→ERender cost — overdraw × material cost. Often larger than sim cost on translucent FX; see the Optimization tab.
Scratch Pad Module HLSL
Scratch Pad Basics
✓Niagara uses a restricted HLSL subset — not the same as Material Custom nodes
✓In the Scratch Pad UI, add input/output map pins and bind each to a Particles.X attribute — the local pin name is what you use in HLSL
✓Parameters.X refers to module-level user parameters (exposed inputs), NOT particle attributes
✓ExecutionIndex gives the current particle index for per-particle logic
✓Use Emitter.LocalSpace / Emitter.Age for emitter-level data
✓Write results via your output pin names — no return statement, no OutputParameters
Scratch Pad Access Pattern
// Bind pins in Scratch Pad UI: Particles.Position → "InPosition"
// In HLSL you use the LOCAL PIN NAME you chose in the UI
float3 Pos      = InPosition;   // bound to Particles.Position
float  Age      = InAge;        // bound to Particles.Age
float  Life     = InLifetime;   // bound to Particles.Lifetime
float  DT       = InDeltaTime;  // bound to Engine.DeltaTime
int    ID       = InUniqueID;   // bound to Particles.UniqueID

// Writing: assign to your output pin names (bound in UI)
OutPosition = Pos + InVelocity * DT; // → Particles.Position
OutColor    = float4(1,0,0,1);        // → Particles.Color

// No return, no OutputParameters — output pins are the writes
Niagara Random Intrinsics

Niagara provides seeded random functions (not HLSL standard):

RandomUInt(seed)Seeded random integer — deterministic per particle
RandomFloat(seed)Seeded random float [0,1]
RandomUnitFloat3(seed)Random direction on unit sphere
Particles.UniqueIDBind to an input pin; use as hash seed for per-particle randomness
Restrictions & Things to Avoid
Niagara HLSL · Never Do This
✗No raw Texture2DSample() calls in Scratch Pad HLSL — use the Texture Sample Data Interface module instead; it wraps the texture correctly for GPU sim
✗No dynamic loop bounds — use [unroll] or fixed iteration counts
✗No ddx / ddy — these are pixel shader only; Niagara sim runs in compute
✗No return statements in Scratch Pad — write to output parameters instead
✗No struct definitions in Scratch Pad — use flat parameter lists
✗Don't assume Material intrinsics — many UE material functions aren't available
✗No UAV writes in standard modules — data flows through typed SRV buffers only
Simulation vs Render Stage
!Simulation HLSL (Spawn/Update) runs as compute — no raw texture calls in HLSL, no derivatives; use Texture Sample Data Interface for texture access
!Render HLSL (Particle Render) differs from simulation HLSL — different available functions
!Data Interface HLSL runs in its own context — check DI docs for available functions
✓Branching is allowed — use BRANCH hint for dynamic branching, FLATTEN to evaluate both
✓Switch statements work in Niagara HLSL — useful for loop mode selection
What IS Available
✓Standard math: lerp, saturate, smoothstep, clamp, abs, sign, pow
✓Vector math: dot, cross, normalize, length, distance
✓Trig: sin, cos, tan, atan2, asin, acos
✓Floor/ceil: floor, ceil, round, frac, fmod
✓Flow control: BRANCH, FLATTEN, UNROLL, LOOP hints
✓Typed buffer reads: Buffer<float> SRV access via index
Stateless Module Patterns · From Real Code
Stateless Particle Struct

Stateless modules use FStatelessParticle + FStatelessParticleContext — different from Scratch Pad:

// Stateless module entry point
void MyModule_Initialize(
    inout FStatelessParticle Particle,
    in FStatelessParticleContext Context)
{
  // Read state
  uint   Idx = Context.ExecIndex;
  float3 Pos = Particle.Position;
  float  Age = Particle.Age;

  // Write state directly
  Particle.Position         = NewPos;
  Particle.PreviousPosition = OldPos;
  Particle.Scale            = float3(1,1,1);
  Particle.Color            = float4(1,0,0,1);
  Particle.MeshOrientation  = NewQuat;
}
GPU Buffer Access Pattern

Stateless modules use typed Buffer<float> SRVs — read 3 or 4 consecutive floats per element:

// Reading float3 from typed float buffer
float3 ReadFloat3FromBuffer(
    Buffer<float> InBuffer, uint Index)
{
  uint Base = Index * 3;
  return float3(
    InBuffer[Base + 0],
    InBuffer[Base + 1],
    InBuffer[Base + 2]);
}

// Reading float4 (color, quaternion)
float4 ReadFloat4FromBuffer(
    Buffer<float> InBuffer, uint Index)
{
  uint Base = Index * 4;
  return float4(
    InBuffer[Base + 0], InBuffer[Base + 1],
    InBuffer[Base + 2], InBuffer[Base + 3]);
}
Indexing Strategies

Common patterns for selecting which array element a particle uses:

// Sequential (round-robin by particle ID)
uint Idx = ParticleID % NumPositions;

// Sequential with clamping (no wrap)
uint Idx = min(ParticleID, NumPositions - 1);

// Random via MurmurHash3 finalizer
uint Hash = ParticleID;
Hash ^= Hash >> 16;
Hash *= 0x85ebca6b;
Hash ^= Hash >> 13;
Hash *= 0xc2b2ae35;
Hash ^= Hash >> 16;
uint Idx = Hash % NumPositions;

// Age-based (interpolate through array)
float T   = NormalizedAge * (float)Count;
uint  Idx = min((uint)T, Count - 1);
Particle Attribute Reference
Particle Attributes · Scratch Pad vs Stateless Access
AttributeNiagara NamespaceStateless StructNotes
**Position**Particles.PositionParticle.PositionBind to Scratch Pad pin; world or local-space per emitter setting
**Previous Position**Particles.PreviousPositionParticle.PreviousPositionLast frame's position — for velocity/trail computation
**Velocity**Particles.VelocityDerived from Pos deltaScratch Pad has explicit velocity; stateless derives it
**Age**Particles.AgeParticle.AgeTime since particle spawned (seconds)
**Lifetime**Particles.LifetimeParticle.LifetimeTotal lifespan (seconds)
**Normalized Age**Particles.Age / Particles.LifetimeParticle.Age / Particle.Lifetime0→1 over lifetime; compute manually in both contexts
**Color**Particles.ColorParticle.Colorfloat4 RGBA
**Scale**Particles.ScaleParticle.Scalefloat3 per-axis scale
**Mesh Orientation**Particles.MeshOrientationParticle.MeshOrientationfloat4 quaternion
**Execution Index**ExecutionIndexContext.ExecIndexPer-particle index for deterministic behavior
**Unique ID**Particles.UniqueIDContext.ParticleIDPersistent ID — use as hash seed for randomness
**Delta Time**Engine.DeltaTimeNot directly availableFrame delta in seconds — bind via Engine namespace
Patterns & Tips
Useful Patterns
✓Random per-particle — bind Particles.UniqueID to an input pin, use as seed with a hash function
✓Custom module — wrap HLSL in a Scratch Pad module for reuse across emitters
✓Quaternion math — multiply quaternions manually (no built-in qmul)
✓Jitter — pre-bake random sphere offsets: RandomUnitFloat3(seed) * Radius
✓Loop modes — implement Once/Loop/PingPong via switch on age-based index
✓Early exit — guard empty arrays: if (NumPositions == 0) return;
Quaternion Multiply (Helper)

No built-in quaternion multiply in Niagara HLSL — use this:

float4 QuatMultiply(float4 Q1, float4 Q2)
{
  return float4(
    Q1.w*Q2.x + Q1.x*Q2.w + Q1.y*Q2.z - Q1.z*Q2.y,
    Q1.w*Q2.y - Q1.x*Q2.z + Q1.y*Q2.w + Q1.z*Q2.x,
    Q1.w*Q2.z + Q1.x*Q2.y - Q1.y*Q2.x + Q1.z*Q2.w,
    Q1.w*Q2.w - Q1.x*Q2.x - Q1.y*Q2.y - Q1.z*Q2.z
  );
}

// Usage: combine rotations
Particle.MeshOrientation =
  QuatMultiply(BaseRot, DeltaRot);
Debugging Tips
!Debug via Color — write intermediate values to Particle.Color to visualize
!Scale debugging — set Particle.Scale = float3(value, value, value) to see ranges
!NaN check — if particles disappear, check for 0/0 or sqrt(negative)
!Stateless emitters are GPU-only — NiagaraStatelessEmitterInstance.cpp:86-93 hard-codes SimTarget = GPUComputeSim; there is no CPU fallback path, so platforms without compute support can't use them
✓Guard empty buffers — always check if (Count == 0) return; before buffer reads
⚡ In-Engine Optimization
Performance Tiers at a Glance
Tier 1 — Effectively Free
✓CCombine emitters — merging 5→1 cuts tick time 30-50% (each adds tick + dispatch + draw setup)
✓FFixed Bounds — enable in System settings; GPU trusts the bounds, skips per-frame recompute
✓FStop unused loops — invisible systems still eat GPU dispatch + CPU
✓FEffect Type asset — attach one per system; enables budget-based culling at zero per-frame cost
Tier 2 — Cheap Wins
✓CLOD via Scalability — configure Low / Medium / High / Epic / Cinematic in Effect Type
✓CDistance culling — particles at 2× distance cost ~4× less fill
✓CTexture atlasing — 4×4 SubUV atlas driven by SubUV Animation module cuts draw calls
Tier 3 — Expensive
!EAvoid CPU per-particle collision — blocking line trace scales linearly; use GPU depth (~3 tex reads) or DF (1 SDF lookup)
!ELight emission — CPU sims only; each particle light = one shadow-casting draw call
!MMany dynamic material params — per-emitter updates flush batching
!EFull-screen translucent sprites — overdraw × material cost multiplies; minimize screen coverage
Tier 4 — Very Expensive
✗EUnbounded GPU sims >1M particles — no fixed bounds = unreliable culling, unbounded memory
✗EUse cutout masks for translucency — 5-10× overdraw = 5-10× material cost
✗E>10 active emitters per actor — each allocates its own dataset + tick entry + dispatch
✗EComplex simulation on mobile — GPU sim is ES3.1-limited; test on lowest target early
GPU vs CPU Simulation
When to Use GPU vs CPU
Decision FactorCPU SimulationGPU Simulation
Particle count sweet spot1 – 100100+ (thousands to millions)
CollisionSync/async physics traces — blocking, scales linearlyDepth buffer (~3 tex reads) or Distance Field (SDF lookup) — parallel, far cheaper
Gameplay events & callbacksYes — full scene query supportNo — compute only, no callbacks
Light emissionYesNo
Module feature setFull — all Niagara modulesLimited — compute-compatible only
Texture samplingVia Data InterfaceVia Texture Sample DI only
Per-frame CPU thread costScales with particle countNear-zero (async GPU dispatch)
Memory pressureSystem RAMVRAM — watch on mobile/consoles
Offline / deterministic renderYes — CPU path availableGPU path only (non-deterministic)
Optimization Rules
DO — Emitter & System
✓Enable Fixed Bounds in System settings — prevents GPU particles from disappearing when emitter actor is off-screen
✓Batch emitters — combine emitters that share the same material into one emitter with SubUV variation
✓Atlas textures — 4×4 SubUV atlas = 16 visual variations, 1 draw call
✓Effect Type asset — define per-quality budgets once and reuse across all systems in the project
✓Deactivate off-screen systems — use Significance Handler to pause systems beyond a set distance
AVOID — Emitter & Render
✗Never-ending loops without culling — idle GPU dispatches drain budget even when nothing is visible
✗Full-screen translucent quads — overdraw × material cost is your largest GPU risk
✗Dynamic bounding boxes — force GPU to re-derive bounds every frame; switch to Fixed Bounds for stable sims
!Keep translucency under 3ms — additive across all FX; check stat gpu
!No Significance Handler without Effect Type — budget overruns go unmanaged; add Effect Type first
Spawning & Renderer Costs
!GPU spawn cap — engine limits spawning to 2M particles per frame across all systems; burst spawning beyond this is silently clamped
!Prefer Sprite over Mesh renderer — mesh computes per-particle vertex data on game thread; CalcMeshUsedParticleCount (default >64) skips hidden indices
!Skeletal Mesh DI is one of the heaviest data interfaces — it binds multiple large SRVs per emitter (index, vertex, skin weights, current + prev bone transforms)
✓Sprite renderer is cheapest — simple billboard quads with minimal per-particle overhead
✓GPU buffer allocation rounds up to 4KB chunks and only shrinks when usage drops below 50%; pre-allocate with MaxAllocationCount in emitter properties to avoid runtime resizing
Overdraw Reduction
✓Cutout / alpha erosion masks — shaped cutout replaces rect sprite, cuts covered pixels 5-10×
✓Reduce particle size — smaller screen footprint = less fill; pair with higher emitter count if needed
✓Distance-based transparency fade — fade particles out before they're too small to be meaningful
!Use Unlit for small particles — Lit translucency is far more expensive; reserve for hero VFX
Scalability & Effect Types
Effect Type Asset — Budget Controls

The Effect Type is a global scalability asset — create one, attach it to every Niagara System via System settings, then configure these per-quality-level budgets:

Max Global Budget UsageCull entire systems once the total Niagara ms budget across the scene is exceeded — the most important setting to set
Max Distance ScaleShrinks cull distance proportionally as the budget fills — near-full budget = more aggressive distance culling automatically
Max Instance Count ScaleGlobally scales down simultaneous particle system instances across all systems that share this Effect Type
Max System Instance CountPer-system instance cap — prevents any one effect from monopolising the budget at the expense of others
Scalability levelsLow / Medium / High / Epic / Cinematic — configure per-level behaviour in Project Settings → Niagara
Significance Handler & Cull Reactions
✓Significance Handler determines which systems stay active when over budget — closest / most visible effects survive
✓Assign a Significance Handler asset in the Effect Type — default handler sorts by distance
✓Custom handlers can weight by screen size, gameplay importance, or camera proximity
!Without an Effect Type, the Significance Handler has no budget thresholds to react to — both must be configured

Cull Reactions (what happens when a system fails its budget check):

DeactivateKill particles gradually, no auto-reactivation — use for one-shot FX
DeactivateImmediateClear all particles instantly, no reactivation — use when popping is acceptable
DeactivateResumeSleep mode — reactivate when passing cull tests again; best for ambient/looping FX
PauseResumeCheapest option — maintains state but stops ticking entirely; no per-frame cost while paused

Scalability update frequency: set to Low (1s) or Medium (0.5s) — Continuous (every frame) adds overhead. The manager parallelises above 50 active instances.

Profiling Tools
stat gpuConsole command — shows GPU ms per pass; watch the Translucency and ParticleSimulation rows specifically
GPU Insights (UE 5.4+)Visual GPU profiler inside Unreal Insights — shows per-system and per-material GPU time on a timeline
Niagara DebuggerWindow → Niagara Debugger — live particle counts, active systems, budget usage per system while in PIE
viewmode shadercomplexityViewport display mode — visualizes per-pixel instruction cost; target green on all particle materials
r.Niagara.GPUCmdBufSafety 1Enables extra GPU safety checks in development builds — disable for shipping builds
🔬 Engine Internals
Data Interfaces · Tips & Gotchas
DI Cost Cheatsheet — Pick by Budget

Data Interfaces are the only way a Niagara script reaches engine data. Costs range from near-free (pre-baked LUTs) to entire GPU passes (async traces, skeletal reads). Bucket them before you add one to an emitter.

TierData InterfacesNotes
Cheap — use freelyCurve, Spline, Vector Field, Simple Counter, Parameter CollectionRead pre-baked LUTs or CPU-side static data. One fetch per particle; near-zero runtime cost.
Medium — budget carefullyArray, Texture 2D, Render Target, Grid2D, Grid3D, Neighbor Grid, CameraAllocate GPU buffers proportional to size; double-buffered grids require a swap per tick. One per emitter is fine, ten is not.
Expensive — hero assets onlySkeletal Mesh, Static Mesh, Async GPU Trace, Collision Query, Export, Data ChannelBind multi-MB SRVs, run compute passes, or force readback/publish paths. Each carries enough hidden state to dominate a frame on its own.
Grid2D / Grid3D · Read-After-Write Hazard
!Grid DIs are double-buffered — a sim-stage write updates the back buffer while reads still hit the front. You never see your own writes inside the same stage
!The buffer swap happens when Execute Stage completes — put reads in the next sim stage, not the writer stage
✗Resizing mid-run corrupts the iteration — sim-stage dispatch args come from GetDispatchArgs() (NiagaraGpuComputeDispatch.cpp:936-945, 1705-1730); resize without rebinding the stage silently desyncs iteration vs. buffer size
!X * Y * Z of a Grid3D must fit in int32 — large grids on mobile hit this before they hit VRAM
Array DI · Sync Mode & Type Restrictions
!GpuSyncMode defaults to None — CPU writes do NOT reach the GPU sim. Flip to CpuToGpu explicitly or the GPU sees a stale array forever
!MaxElements is a hard cap — overruns clamp silently, no warning in the log
✗Atomic ops (AtomicAdd, AtomicMin, etc.) only work on int and uint array types; float arrays compile but the ops are no-ops
✓Prefer Array DIs over raw StructuredBuffer — the DI handles proxy lifetime, GPU mirror, and safe resize; raw buffers leak across reloads
Collision Query · Silent Stage Dependencies
!CPU path runs sync physics line traces — blocks the game thread and scales linearly with particle count
!GPU Depth collision needs the depth buffer populated before the tick — works in PostOpaque tick groups, silently returns nothing in PrePhysics
!GPU Distance Field collision requires r.GenerateMeshDistanceFields=1 in project settings and individual meshes marked for DF generation — no error if missing, just empty hits
✗Mobile / ES3.1 has no distance field support — the DI compiles but returns no hits; always test on target hardware
✓Prefer Depth collision (~3 texture reads) for environmental bounces; reserve Distance Field for shape-aware gameplay queries
Data Channel DI · Frame-Scoped Pub/Sub
✓Niagara Data Channels are a one-frame pub/sub bus — writers publish this frame, readers consume either this frame or next depending on tick order
!fx.Niagara.DataChannels.ForceReadPrevFrame overrides every NDC reader globally — one forced emitter drags the whole project into prev-frame reads (NiagaraDataInterfaceDataChannelRead.cpp:50-64)
!fx.Niagara.DataChannels.ForceReadTickGroup pins every NDC read into one tick group — breaks parallel tick-group load balancing for every NDC consumer
✗Data Channels are not persistent — if the reader ticks before the writer this frame, it sees nothing. Use tick prerequisites or explicit PrevFrame reads
Skeletal Mesh, Export & Render Target
!Skeletal Mesh DI is the heaviest built-in — binds index, vertex, skin weights, and current + previous bone transforms per emitter. Its RWGuard is reentrant-safe but not recursive — two emitters sampling the same mesh from different threads contend for the lock
!Export DI (GPU → CPU readback) is capped by fx.Niagara.GpuReadbackMaxBytes; overruns are silently dropped, and the readback fence adds one frame of latency
!Render Target DI — resolution is per-system and persists across the render target's lifetime; shrinking it mid-session does not reclaim VRAM until the next system reset
✓Curve DI bakes to a texture LUT at editor time — runtime cost is a single texture fetch. Editing the curve asset triggers a rebake on next PIE; cooked builds use the baked LUT only
✓Spline DI LUT is immutable per-instance — move the spline actor and particles follow, but reshape the spline and already-spawned particles see stale positions
Engine Gotchas · Source-Verified
Interpolated Spawn · 2-Frame Parameter Validity
ProblemParameters read on frame 0 of an interpolated-spawn emitter are stale — velocity-based spawn math produces garbage on the very first frame.
WhyThe engine flips parameter stores via FlipParameterBuffers(); ParametersValid only sets on the second flip. Scoped to interpolated-spawn emitters — regular emitters don't hit this. NiagaraEmitter.cpp:670
FixEither clamp spawn to frame >= 1, or switch off interpolated spawning if you don't need velocity blending between spawn events.
DetectFirst-frame particles spawn in the wrong place or with wrong velocity; subsequent frames look correct. Log bParametersValid in the Update script to confirm.
ProblemInterpolated-spawn emitters always tick the update script, even when idle — per-frame cost never drops to zero.
WhyUsesInterpolatedSpawning() sets bGpuAlwaysRunParticleUpdateScript = true. NiagaraEmitter.cpp:670
FixDisable Interpolated Spawning unless the emitter genuinely needs frame-to-frame velocity blending. For ambient looping FX this is almost always unnecessary.
Detectstat gpu — Niagara update time stays flat while visible particle count trends to zero.
ProblemFlipping the Interpolated Spawning checkbox without recompiling silently desyncs InterpolatedSpawnMode from the compiled script — common cause of "velocity blending stopped working."
WhyThe engine silently resets InterpolatedSpawnMode to NoInterpolation when the flag disagrees with the compiled state, with no warning. NiagaraEmitter.cpp:854-860
FixAfter toggling the checkbox, hit Apply or recompile the emitter. Don't just save the asset.
DetectSpawned particles with velocity inheritance suddenly pop to the emitter origin instead of smoothly interpolating — and no log entry explains why.
GPU Dispatch · X→Y Silent Remap at 65,535
ProblemShader code that recovers a linear thread ID from SV_DispatchThreadID.x alone produces wrong indices above ~4M particles.
WhyWhen DispatchCount.X exceeds GRHIMaxDispatchThreadGroupsPerDimension.X (65,535 on most RHIs), the dispatcher silently redistributes groups onto Y. NiagaraGpuComputeDispatch.cpp:1775-1802
FixAlways use DispatchThreadId.x + DispatchThreadId.y * DispatchCount.x to recover the linear index, never just .x.
DetectSim starts reading the wrong particle slots above ~4M particles with no warning. Debug by capping SpawnRate and watching for position discontinuities.
ProblemFree-ID assignment lags spawn by one frame — particles spawned in frame N have INDEX_NONE for their persistent ID until frame N+1.
WhyInstance count buffer updates run in PostOpaque tick group. NiagaraGpuComputeDispatch.cpp:223-228, 514-518
FixNever read a particle's persistent ID on the same frame it spawned. Gate any ID-lookup logic on PersistentID != INDEX_NONE.
DetectCode that maps a particle to a spawn event (e.g. damage numbers bound to hit particles) misses the first frame of each spawn.
ProblemEvent spawn overflow is silent — a 5,000-hit burst against a 1,000-particle buffer silently drops 4,000.
WhyEvent handlers clamp spawn count to remaining buffer space with no log entry.
FixSize the event spawn buffer (MaxAllocationCount) against your worst-case burst. Prefer GPU event systems with persistent buffers over CPU event handlers for bursty gameplay events.
DetectExpected particle bursts look visually thinner than debug draws of the underlying events. Check emitter max-allocation setting vs. the event frequency.
Scalability Lag · Up to 1 Second Behind Camera

The scalability manager is rate-limited by three per-significance CVars and a per-frame cap (NiagaraScalabilityManager.cpp:10-22, 38-77):

fx.NiagaraScalabilityUpdateTime_LowDefault 1.0 s — for inactive / distant systems
fx.NiagaraScalabilityUpdateTime_MediumDefault 0.5 s — for moderately significant systems
fx.NiagaraScalabilityUpdateTime_HighDefault 0.25 s — for high-significance systems (hero VFX)
fx.ScalabilityMaxUpdatesPerFrameDefault 50 — caps how many instances re-evaluate significance per frame
ProblemFast camera cuts pay old-LOD cost for the full Low cadence (1 s default) before culling catches up.
WhyThe scalability manager only re-evaluates each instance at its cadence — on a cinematic cut, everything in the old frustum remains at its old LOD for up to a full second.
FixBudget for it on cinematic cuts, or drop fx.NiagaraScalabilityUpdateTime_Low to 0.25 s before the cut and restore after.
DetectGPU spike on the first second after a camera cut, then drop. Correlate with stat gpu timings against camera-cut timing.
ProblemUNiagaraSystem::bNeedsSignificanceCull is opt-in per system — without it, the scalability manager treats all instances as flat, with no distance ranking.
WhyEffect Types alone don't enable it — the per-system flag must be set separately. NiagaraScalabilityManager.cpp:262-266, 285-290
FixEnable Needs Significance Cull on every System you want distance-sorted. Audit existing systems — this is frequently missed.
DetectAll instances of a system get culled together rather than nearest-first when budget fills. Budget-overflow behavior looks random rather than distance-ordered.
Low-Latency Translucency · Per-Emitter VRAM Double
ProblemVRAM cost doubles per GPU emitter — dozens of GPU emitters silently cost hundreds of MB.
Whyfx.NiagaraGpuLowLatencyTranslucencyEnabled defaults to 1. It allocates a second particle data buffer per GPU emitter so translucent materials can read current-frame sim data regardless of tick stage. NiagaraGpuComputeDispatch.cpp:71-77
FixOn VRAM-constrained platforms (mobile, Switch) set the CVar to 0. Trade-off: PostOpaqueRender emitters sample 1-frame-old positions — visible as subtle smear on fast particles. There is no per-emitter override.
DetectUnexpected VRAM growth proportional to GPU emitter count. Check with stat vram or RenderDoc — look for 2× expected particle buffer allocations.
State Machine · Disabled is Terminal
ProblemAsync operations can make RequestedExecutionState = Complete while ActualExecutionState = Active. Branching on Requested is unreliable.
WhyTwo state slots — Requested is a hint, Actual is authoritative. Requested changes synchronously; Actual follows after async teardown completes.
FixAlways branch on ActualExecutionState, never RequestedExecutionState, for any gameplay decision.
DetectSpawn checks that gate on state sometimes fire one frame late; async particle systems look "stuck pending" in the debugger.
ProblemOnce ActualExecutionState == Disabled, the only recovery is Reset(EResetMode::ReInit). All other transitions respect this invariant.
FixIf a system enters Disabled and you need it back, call Reset. Don't try to force Activate — it silently no-ops.
DetectActivate() returns but the system never starts. Check ActualExecutionState in the Niagara Debugger.
ProblemScalability culling ≠ destruction — culled emitters return ShouldTick() == false but stay allocated. A system with all emitters culled can still reach Complete.
FixIf you need a system to survive culling, gate Complete logic on visible particle count > 0 rather than on culling state.
DetectSystems that should loop forever sometimes unload after being off-screen for a while. Check Complete-on-disable settings.
ProblemWarm-up ticks are invisible to game logic — a 100-frame warmup can spawn thousands of particles before the first visible frame.
WhySetWarmupSettings() runs N pre-ticks before activation; warmup particles don't fire events or update bounds.
FixKeep warmup under 30 frames for gameplay-relevant FX. For pure ambient decor, higher is fine but budget the initial cost on spawn.
DetectSpawn-time GPU spike disproportionate to visible particle count on system activation. Check WarmupTicks in the asset.
UE5 Shader Conversion Cheatsheet · GitHub Home · Materials · Shaders MIT License · Community Edition
